sentences.text: Not that I remember.
No, not that I remember.
Oh, speak a little louder, right?
Oh, sure.
[LAUGHTER]
[LAUGHS] No, not that I know of.
You want to take that out, what I said between on that?
Yeah.
I don't think they practiced it much.
I don't think so.
No, I don't think so.
Because I did not know before we meet them.
We met them here in our house, you know.
And I-- we sometimes discussed things, you know, about Christianity and the Jewish belief.
And we had good talks each other.
We understood each other and respected each other beliefs.
And--
Yes.
We had-- we felt strongly about the difference in Christ, what we believe in, you know, and the Jewish faith, think still messiah is coming.
And the one thing I remember that Mops said, if that is true, then all the rabbis would be wrong.
Yeah, that's true, too.
You know, the rabbis don't believe in that, either.
And that was sometimes discussions we had, that was always really even-- really pleasant and interesting.
And we, of course, never tried to get them, you know, over the other side.
Those were the [INAUDIBLE] of our discussions.
Not much.
Nobody knew much about it.
They only were told they were going to a-- work camps.
They needed people to work, and they picked them up.
And they had to bring clothes with them, warm clothes.
And the-- the ones who were picked up from the street or at their houses-- nothing.
But they were only told, you are going to work somewhere else.
And that's all they knew when they left.
And the stories, what came out later, of course, it was unimaginable.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
What did you see?
Yeah.
Fear.
If you think about it, that they come to your house and they get you out of the house-- even a child.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] scolding, names, swines, whatever.
They-- they-- it was terrible, what we heard later.
So-- but even besides that, to get picked up, by itself, can you imagine how that feels?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
That is just horrible.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Well, what-- what he did-- when a few months they were there, it was getting light.
We had to do something.
Because you walk out the door, you're in the yard or whatever.
You had to do something.
And then he said, well, I think-- and then he spun a story.

He-- the only thing we could do, invite them over-- the Nazis-- our neighbors, invite them over in a friendly way, and meet them, and tell a fabricated [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's what we were going to do.
And he said, but my face is so-- his face was really Middle East, you know?
Really a Middle Easterner.
She-- no.
She had nice, brown eyes, and he had beautiful blue eyes.
He was a Jewish man with blue eyes-- a very, very pleasant laugh.
He always had laughs.
And he said, I have a story.
So invited them over, and they came.
And I never forget the man's face when he saw Max.
He knew.
He expected it.
And down we came in in conversation.
And at first you start with the weather, right, and all little things about war and everything.
And then he started about telling his work.
He was in-- in-- he worked for a-- oh, he made the false teeth.
What is it he made?
The-- dentals.
That's what it was.
And then Max told him the story, that he said, you know what is bothering me so much?
That I look so Jewish.
He said, but my ancestors came from Germany in a little place over there.
And they had the archives through my ancestors because I am not Jewish.
And that man, we knew, he was very interested in-- how do you call it again?
I cannot think of the word-- when they-- like our ancestors.
Genealogy?
Yeah.
Right.
He was very interested in that.
And he did that.
Since we knew that, we made up the story.
And he made up the story.
He says, I know you are into the work.
I would like to know my ancestors.
And then I can even show you.
And he was so-- and because he said so, he told him, I look so Jewish, he believed him.
And he did the work.
He did the work.
But Max knew the little town was bombed flat.
And he was as Dutch as we were, so it was all a made-up story.
There were no archives anyway there.
But he could nothing say about it, because the town was bombed, and we did not know that.
But he knew.
And they did believe him.
So then that-- that fear was--
And we visited sometimes more.
And we even-- it was a cute thing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Let me see.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
Yeah, they got a baby.
And-- oh, what was it now?
Oh, and he took pictures with the baby on Mab's lap.
The Nazi had the baby.
The baby on Mab's lap.
And it was little low cutout, like she was feeding.
And you had to laugh, she said-- to laugh.
She said, he took a picture of my-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] breast, whatever, you know.
So far went it, you know.
They trusted us so much.
It was really an idea, but it never-- they never thought of us, really, hide us.
Six months.
Because my brother-- one day, we heard the news that my brother Simon was picked up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It happened that they found a-- my brother's identification card on a Jewish man who was picked up.
It was my brother's name.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, of course, they came to my brother's house and showed him.
My-- my brother had a false one, another one.
Gave his identification as Simon--
Simon Bastiaanse on the Jewish man.
And he was picked up.
And he gave his papers, and they found out it was not him.
And it was a card for my brother.
So on one night, it was, I think, also on the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] night of the-- yeah.
I had first-- the day before, I had visited my sister-in-law, who was-- her birthday was on the 10th of May, too.
And I left, and the next morning we heard that he was picked up that same night.
I didn't know my brother had people in his house when I was there with my son.
They were hidden, too, like we had them hidden.
But they were not ever in a closet or whatever.
And so they came in the middle of the night, and they banged on the door.
And they stormed in the house.
And they showed a picture.
And then they said, we want to search your home.
If they had only picked up my brother, it would have been-- you know.
But they searched the house.
And the people were in a closet, and they found the people in his house, so all three of them there.
A bunch of them, you know, scolding and beating and gave them bloody noses and everything.
They came in to-- they took them to their headquarters.
And they put my brother-- [SIGHS] they had interrogation for several days.
And then they put him in a jail in [NON-ENGLISH], resort place in Holland.
And they put him in strict seclusion.
And he was there for three months, never saw people, never got something to read.
The only thing he saw was a hand that gave him his food.
And I heard they do that to make them crazy.
But he did not.
And then when that happened, we got hot feet, too.
We had to find a place for them, because we didn't know if he could take the interrogations and tell them what was going on here in our house at the time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there were so little hiding places left, because the war was almost three years going on already.
So it was very hard.
And after about, oh, four or five weeks, he-- Max with his identification, he went on a train and went to a friend next door where he lived and told him about if he could help him out.
And sure enough, about a week after, he got a message that he found something in a little house in a different province.
It was Brabant.
So that is what happened.
And Cor went with him yet, you know, with the luggage and so on.
And it was in-- I think it was June 5 that they left from our house.
And in that place where they were, it was very small.
And they lived there until the war was over.
So they lived there until-- yeah, the other year, in May again.
That was in '44-- no, that was in '43 they left.
So they lived there about two years more.
And we didn't know they were still alive, because there was so much fighting all over.
We didn't even know they had made it.
But they made it.
When they left your house, that again was on bicycles?
Or how did they leave?
Did they have suitcases?
No, at that time I think the train still was-- they took the train, I think.
Yeah.
But Cor will know.
I think they took the train.
It was still running.
I am talking too soft, huh?
Not that I knew of, because nobody tells you.
It could be very possibly.
I think there were church groups who did it.
I-- I'm almost sure.
No.
We didn't know.
It could easily be.
You don't know.
If your neighbors don't know what you are doing, how would you know a church group were doing?
Nobody knew.
I didn't know about my brother, what he did.
My parents didn't know.
My parents didn't know about us.
Only if so, but we never ask any help, except what we tried to find another place.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was on my uncle and my aunt and other friend of us, and he couldn't do it, either.
But for the rest, you cannot go to your neighbors or whatever or to regular friends.
No.
They don't know.
Nobody knew.
Only when sometimes my mom came to visit, and we arranged it with my brother, who lived next door, if they come, knock on the wall and said, who is there?
That we knew that my mom was visiting me, too.
You could go from the back into my house.
So it happened once.
My mom came, and my brother, bang, bang, bang, bang on the door.
Mom is here.
And she will be right over!
And we were just sitting on the dining room table, eating supper.
And I went the plates.
They went all over the place, in cabinets or whatever, only two plates from us.
And they went upstairs, all the way up to the attic, and closed the thing.
And they were sitting there.
And my mother came in, and there were just two plates on the table.
We were finishing our dinner.
Those things-- and it happened more often, of course, because we knew-- all of a sudden somebody came, they-- woosh-- they went up in the attic.
Not to look for them, but once, what I was trying to tell you before, that was on a Saturday.
My husband went for his paycheck to the city, and he had our little son.
You know, we took every week Harro, our son, was allowed to go for a little trip and see the grandmas and so on.
So he always went with him.
Yeah.
And that was in 1944.
Our second son was born in August '44.
And I think he was about six or seven weeks old.
And it was on a Saturday morning that my husband went into the city.
And he stayed away for a few hours, used to.
And all of a sudden-- I was making a little pudding in the kitchen, and I heard a bang, bang on the door.
And I think, who's that now?
I walked to the door and opened the door.
And I got frozen-- two Germans, fully, you know, with his rifle and his bullets are out.
He is all fully equipped-- two of them.
Were they Wehrmacht or SS or-- do you know if they were SS Germans or regular army Germans?
Army Germans, I would say.
So they came in.
And he told me to search my house for a radio.
And we had a radio.
We had it hidden in the attic underneath.
And every night, we used to go, 7 o'clock, whatever, to try to BBC, at that time, to get the news, what was going on.
That was always fairly hard, because there was a lot of static.
They did that on purpose, the Germans, the static, so people could not listen to the radio and heard the news from outside.
Because you were so isolated from the whole world, really.
And so it was there.
And they came in, I think, oh, ooh, ooh, ooh.
What now?
And [SIGHS] they came in, and I walked to my kitchen.
And number three came in.
He came in from the back.
And he started to search.
He searched the bread box and the cabinets and-- and-- and all over the place.
And then they wanted to-- they told me, said, I'm looking for a radio.
I said, we delivered the radio.
We don't have a radio.
He said, can I have your-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] receipt?
I said, yes, you can have it.
And I-- the book where I had the receipts, and believed I could not find it.
And we had a receipt, but I could not find it.
I was too nervous.
And I think, you know what?
Let him look for himself.
I was getting mad, really.
And I told him, you do it yourself.
You find it.
What was the receipt?
The receipt that we had a radio delivered.
We had to deliver our radios.
Yes.
We had to.
And we gave them an old one, like many people did.
And the good one we kept.
And we had that in the attic.
So I had a receipt.
But I couldn't find it.
So I said, you look through it.
So he got the privilege to look even through my paperwork.
Then I took another chance.
I think, he takes that.
How fast you can think?
At the little baby dresser, I had a drawer, and there were illegal papers in, because so much information from illegal papers that was given from hand to hand.
If the neighbor had an illegal paper, you want to know what happened, and you give it to one of them.
Illegal newspapers.
Yeah.
Yeah, illegal newspaper.
And they were in the drawer.
I think, before he do that, I'd let the drawer open.
It was a big chance I took.
But when they see an open drawer-- they think, well, there is nothing there or else they wouldn't leave an open drawer.
And I went to the baby.
I pick up the baby.
And they looked at me, and they followed me.
And they looked over there.
And he couldn't read Dutch anyway.
So when they looked at that open drawer, it was OK.
So it was a good thing, without even going upstairs.
Those big boots, you know, the three of them-- no, the two of them.
The one stayed downstairs.
And they went into the bathrooms, and I heard them walking, opening closets and everything.
And then they went upstairs.
There was a little ladder, and it was a trap door.
And all of a sudden, I heard yelling in German.
And the one who was downstairs, he ran upstairs.
And I thought, is it.
He ran upstairs, and my blood ran cold.
And I picked up the baby, and I stood for the window.
I was just praying, my neighbors, please tell Cor.
Just say, Cor, don't come home.
Because they were looking at our window, what was going on.
It was only our house, what they are searching.
Because they knew.
Because we know now what it was.
Across the street, there was a [NON-ENGLISH], an officer, who had taken a room from the people.
They did that.
And it was a front room.
And I think he watched our house and saw sometimes neighbors going in around 7 o'clock.
That is what I-- he was the one, I think, who got the idea.
And that's what I'm sure it was.
So I was standing in front of the window, and people were looking.
And I think, oh-- and my bother next door-- I hope they understand that Cor went to the city-- like brainwaves, you know.
And then they came down.
And I had my baby.
And I was so uptight.
And I heard them clomping down the stair, and I knew they had the-- the radio in their hands.
I just knew it.
And they found it because they went upstairs, and they were yelling.
And they came downstairs empty-handed.
They didn't find it.
And they were very disappointed.
They clomped out without a word and slammed the door.
And the whole day after that, I saw it until dark.
I saw they were going back and forth in the front of the house-- back and forth, and back and forth, still watching the house.
So my husband came, but he never came to the front.
And he went around the house, and he came in and said, what is that?
So I told him the story.
He couldn't believe it, of course.
So my husband was safe, and little son, and we were.
That was really experience.
Where was the radio?
Between-- in the-- we lift a beam, or a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had it in the floor, between the--
Yeah, the floorboards.
There was it.
Yeah.
Oh, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.
That was true.
That was done-- one day, they came to pick up our bikes.
They wanted bikes with air tires, because we most had almost solid tires.
And we saw them walking from one house to the other, crossing the streets.
And we know our priest.
He still visited his parishioners with a-- on a bicycle.
And he was a dear man.
And he was involved, too, I think, in underground, because they were picking up men under 40.
They did that for several times, picking up men for labor in Germany.
They just pick them up from the street or whatever.
Once it happened they picked up, I think, 5,000 men and routed them.
Just pick them up and bring them to the workplaces in Germany or wherever.
So we saw them walking over there again.
And we thought on the bicycle of the priest.
And my husband and my brother, my neighbor, they went to his house.
They picked up the bicycle, and we had a little brook on the side, and we dumped it in the brook.
And they came closer and closer and closer, and there was nothing.
And they left.
And we picked it up again, and we gave it back to the priest.
Amen, I said.
[LAUGHS] But the priest was a very nice person.
He seemed to be in underground, too, because they were picking up men under 40, and my husband was not 40.
So he said, I can change your identification, 10 years older.
You are over 40.
And that was fine.
My husband grew a mustache, and he looked older.
So until after-- until the war was over, he had an identification.
He was 10 years older.
And we kept him out of trouble.
Yeah.
Pastor-- yeah, Pastor Bax it was, B-A-X, I think.
Pastor Bax.
They were-- he was six-- three months in isolation.
And after that, I wrote the story what he told us ourself.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was taken from his cell and given a shave.
And the barber said, if you say a word, it's your consequences.
Don't say a word.
OK.
So they shaved him, and they got other clothes.
And they transported him to a concentration camp in Belgium.
And there, he worked hard labor-- very hard labor and very cold.
They did not have the right clothes.
They were very hungry.
A few slices of bread for a whole day hard work was even more than enough, was even too much, according to the commander.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were punished, too, of course.
And every morning at 5 or 6 o'clock, there was appel in the very cold, cold weather.
And he survived that camp and the hard work.
He was once beaten, too, because it was so cold-- oh, then they went to another concentration camp.
And they had to bring cement, bags of cement, to-- carrying, not on-- on a wheelbarrow-- carrying from one place to the other.
And they were working, and worked for air-- airfields, or whatever.
I don't remember what it was.
But it was the idea that they had to carry those 50 pounds of bags, and sometimes on the bare feet or whatever.
Their hands were more blood then.
But they had to work.
And it was so cold.
So they think, you know, what we do?
We take the cement bags, and we make like clothes, you know, like put holes for the arms, and we cut out, put our heads.
That's not so cold.
And there they put their poor clothes, what they wore, over it.
They cover it.
And they found out.
And it was punishable.
They found two.
They found out.
And they got their punishment.
And that was done in a way that everybody could see it.
And they had to bend over a buckhorse.
And then the one who beat them, they sang the song.
Every beat, they sang a song, every beat.
And they had to count the beatings themselves in German.
And if they make a mistake, they start all over again.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But according to my brother, he said the beating was-- the humiliation was worse than the beating.
Humiliated.
[SIGHS] Oh, Vucht, I think-- V-U-C-H-T.
I think it was Vucht, that one.
